instruction: How does the stack work in Magic: The Gathering?
output: The stack is a zone where spells and abilities wait to resolve. When a spell is cast or an ability is activated/triggered, it goes on top of the stack. Objects on the stack resolve one at a time from the top down (last in, first out). Both players must pass priority in succession before the top item resolves. This means the most recently added spell or ability resolves first.

instruction: When do players receive priority in MTG?
output: The active player (the player whose turn it is) receives priority at the beginning of most steps and phases, after turn-based actions and triggered abilities are handled. A player also receives priority after a spell or ability resolves, and after they cast a spell, activate an ability, or take a special action. No player receives priority during the untap step, and players usually don't get priority during the cleanup step.

instruction: Can I respond to my own spell on the stack?
output: Yes. After you cast a spell, you receive priority again before your opponent does (rule 117.3c). This means you can cast another instant or activate an ability in response to your own spell. For example, you could cast Brainstorm and then respond to it with another instant before Brainstorm resolves.

instruction: What happens when both players pass priority in succession?
output: When all players pass priority in succession without taking any actions, the top spell or ability on the stack resolves. If the stack is empty when all players pass, the current step or phase ends and the next one begins. This is how the game advances through its phases and how spells and abilities ultimately resolve.

instruction: How does split second work?
output: Split second is a static ability that functions while the spell with split second is on the stack. While such a spell is on the stack, players can't cast other spells or activate abilities that aren't mana abilities. However, triggered abilities still trigger and are put on the stack normally, mana abilities can still be activated, and special actions (like unmorphing a creature) can still be taken. Split second doesn't make a spell uncounterable - it just severely limits responses.

instruction: Can I activate abilities while a spell is on the stack?
output: Yes. Whenever you have priority, you can activate abilities regardless of what's on the stack (rule 117.1b). The only exception is if a spell with split second is on the stack, which prevents activating non-mana abilities. Activated abilities go on the stack on top of whatever is already there and will resolve before the spells beneath them.

instruction: How do triggered abilities interact with the stack?
output: Triggered abilities can trigger at any time, including while spells are being cast or resolving. However, they don't go on the stack immediately. Each time a player would receive priority, all abilities that have triggered but haven't been put on the stack are placed on the stack. The active player puts their triggered abilities on the stack first, then the non-active player in turn order (APNAP). Each player chooses the order of their own triggers.

instruction: What is LIFO (last in, first out) in the context of MTG?
output: LIFO refers to the stack's resolution order: the last spell or ability added to the stack is the first one to resolve. For example, if Player A casts Lightning Bolt and Player B responds with Counterspell, the Counterspell (last in) resolves first, countering the Lightning Bolt before it can resolve. This ordering is fundamental to how interaction works in Magic.

instruction: Can I respond to Show and Tell being cast in Legacy?
output: Yes. When your opponent casts Show and Tell, it goes on the stack and you receive priority. You can cast instants like Force of Will or Daze to counter it, activate abilities, or take other instant-speed actions. However, once Show and Tell starts resolving, both players simultaneously choose and reveal a permanent card - you cannot respond between the choice and the placement of permanents.

instruction: How does Daze work on the stack in Legacy?
output: Daze is an instant with an alternative cost of returning an Island you control to its owner's hand instead of paying its mana cost of {1}{U}. When cast, it goes on the stack and counters a target spell unless its controller pays {1}. Your opponent then has priority and can choose to pay {1} to prevent their spell from being countered. The key is that bouncing your Island is part of the cost, so it happens before your opponent can respond.

instruction: When can Force of Will be cast in Legacy?
output: Force of Will is an instant, so it can be cast any time you have priority. Its alternative cost lets you pay 1 life and exile a blue card from your hand instead of paying {3}{U}{U}. This means you can counter a spell even when you're tapped out, on any player's turn. This is what makes Force of Will the premier counterspell in Legacy - it provides interaction even when you have no mana available.

instruction: If my opponent casts a spell and I cast Force of Will, can they respond to my Force of Will?
output: Yes. After you cast Force of Will, your opponent receives priority and can respond. They could cast their own counterspell, another Force of Will, or any other instant-speed action. Force of Will goes on top of the stack above their spell, and all players must pass priority before Force of Will resolves. The stack allows for chains of responses between players.

instruction: Can multiple triggered abilities go on the stack at the same time?
output: Yes. If multiple triggered abilities trigger simultaneously, they are placed on the stack when a player would next receive priority. The active player puts all their triggered abilities on the stack in any order they choose, then the non-active player does the same (APNAP order - Active Player, Non-Active Player). Since the last ability placed resolves first, the ordering can be strategically important.

instruction: What happens if I cast an instant in response to a triggered ability?
output: Your instant goes on top of the stack above the triggered ability and will resolve first. For example, if your opponent's Delver of Secrets triggers to reveal the top card, you can respond with Brainstorm to rearrange the top of your opponent's - wait, Brainstorm affects your library. More relevantly, if a triggered ability targets something you control, you can respond with protection or removal before it resolves.

instruction: Can I cast Stifle in response to a triggered ability on the stack?
output: Yes. Stifle counters a target activated or triggered ability. When a triggered ability is put on the stack, players receive priority and can respond. You can cast Stifle targeting that triggered ability to counter it. In Legacy, this is commonly used to counter fetchland activations, Storm triggers, Standstill triggers, and the cascade trigger from cards like Shardless Agent.

instruction: How does priority work during the resolution of a spell?
output: No player has priority while a spell or ability is resolving (rule 117.2e). Players cannot cast spells or activate non-mana abilities during resolution. The spell or ability's instructions are carried out in order, and players may be asked to make choices, but they cannot interrupt the resolution with other spells or abilities. Priority is only granted again after the spell or ability finishes resolving.

instruction: Can I cast an instant between two spells my opponent casts in a row?
output: Yes. Your opponent cannot cast two spells without you having a chance to respond. After your opponent casts a spell, they receive priority first (rule 117.3c), so they could cast another instant in response to their own spell. But if they pass priority instead, you receive it and can respond. If they want to cast a second spell, you'll always have had a chance to respond to the first.

instruction: Does a spell fizzle if all its targets become illegal?
output: Yes. When a spell or ability tries to resolve, the game checks if its targets are still legal. If all targets are illegal, the spell or ability is removed from the stack without resolving and none of its effects happen (it is 'countered by the game rules'). If a spell has multiple targets and at least one is still legal, it resolves but does nothing to the illegal targets.

instruction: Can I hold priority after casting a spell to cast another spell before my opponent can respond?
output: Yes. After you cast a spell, you receive priority (rule 117.3c). You can immediately cast another instant or activate an ability before passing priority to your opponent. In competitive play, you must explicitly announce that you're holding priority, as the default assumption is that you pass priority after casting a spell. This is relevant in Legacy for Storm combos where you want to stack multiple spells.

instruction: How does the stack work with Storm in Legacy?
output: When you cast a spell with Storm, the Storm trigger goes on the stack on top of the original spell. When the Storm trigger resolves, it creates copies of the spell for each other spell cast that turn. These copies are put directly on the stack and are not 'cast,' so they don't increase the storm count. The copies resolve one at a time from top to bottom before the original spell resolves.

instruction: If I Surgical Extraction a card in response to an Entomb, what happens?
output: Entomb must resolve first before you can target the card it finds. When your opponent casts Entomb, it goes on the stack. You can respond, but the card hasn't been searched for yet. Once Entomb resolves, the card is put into the graveyard, and then the active player receives priority. At that point you can cast Surgical Extraction targeting the card just entombed, exiling it and all other copies.

instruction: Can Thalia, Guardian of Thraben's tax be paid with the extra mana from a Lion's Eye Diamond?
output: No, not in the way you might hope. Lion's Eye Diamond's ability can only be activated when you could cast an instant, and activating it requires discarding your hand. The mana from LED cannot be used to pay for spells in your hand because you discard your hand as part of the cost. However, if a spell is already on the stack (like Infernal Tutor), you can crack LED in response and use the mana later.

instruction: What happens if both players have triggered abilities that trigger at the same time?
output: When multiple triggered abilities trigger simultaneously, they go on the stack in APNAP order (Active Player, Non-Active Player). The active player puts all their triggered abilities on the stack first in any order they choose, then the non-active player does the same. Since the stack resolves top-down (LIFO), the non-active player's triggers resolve first. Each player chooses the order of their own simultaneous triggers.

instruction: Can I respond to my opponent activating a fetchland in Legacy?
output: Yes. A fetchland activation (like Flooded Strand's 'pay 1 life, sacrifice: search for a Plains or Island') is an activated ability that goes on the stack. After your opponent activates it, you receive priority and can respond. In Legacy, players commonly respond with Stifle to counter the ability (the fetchland is already sacrificed as a cost, so they lose the land), or with Wasteland targeting another land while they're tapped out.

instruction: How does priority work when my opponent casts Hymn to Tourach?
output: When your opponent casts Hymn to Tourach, it goes on the stack. You receive priority and can respond with instants or abilities before it resolves. Once you pass priority and it resolves, you discard two cards at random - you cannot respond between the random selection and the discard because no player has priority during resolution. If you have Brainstorm, you should cast it in response to hide cards on top of your library.

instruction: Can I float mana and then let a spell resolve?
output: Yes. You can activate mana abilities at any time you have priority or whenever you need to pay a cost. If you tap lands for mana and then pass priority, the mana stays in your mana pool until the end of the current step or phase. So you can tap lands for mana, let your opponent's spell resolve, and still have that mana available to use afterward (as long as you're still in the same step or phase).

instruction: What happens to spells on the stack if a player loses the game?
output: In a two-player game, when a player loses, the game ends immediately. In multiplayer, when a player leaves the game, all spells and abilities controlled by that player are removed from the stack, and any objects they own that aren't on the battlefield cease to exist. Permanents they control that they don't own are exiled. The game continues for remaining players.

instruction: Can I Veil of Summer in response to a Thoughtseize to protect my hand?
output: Yes. When your opponent casts Thoughtseize targeting you, you can respond by casting Veil of Summer. Veil resolves first, giving you hexproof from blue and black (which doesn't help since Thoughtseize targets a player) - but more importantly, it says you can't be the target of blue or black spells your opponents control this turn and it draws you a card. Since Thoughtseize is black, it will be countered for having an illegal target.

instruction: If a spell with multiple targets has some targets become illegal, does it still resolve?
output: Yes, as long as at least one target remains legal. The spell resolves and does as much as it can. It performs its effects on the remaining legal targets and ignores the illegal ones. For example, if Kolaghan's Command targets a creature and an artifact, and the creature is sacrificed in response, the artifact mode still resolves. If ALL targets become illegal, the entire spell is countered by game rules.

instruction: Can I activate Aether Vial while a spell with split second is on the stack?
output: No. Aether Vial's activated ability is not a mana ability, so it cannot be activated while a spell with split second is on the stack. Split second prevents players from casting spells and activating non-mana abilities. The only things you can do while a split second spell is on the stack are activate mana abilities, take special actions (like unmorphing), and let triggered abilities go on the stack.

instruction: How do I respond to Show and Tell if my opponent puts Emrakul into play?
output: You cannot respond between the resolution of Show and Tell and Emrakul entering the battlefield - it all happens during resolution. However, you also get to put a permanent onto the battlefield with Show and Tell. You could put in your own threat, a Karakas to bounce Emrakul, or an Oblivion Ring to exile it. After Show and Tell finishes resolving and both permanents are on the battlefield, you then receive priority and can take further actions.

instruction: What is the difference between 'counter target spell' and 'counter target activated or triggered ability'?
output: Spells and abilities are different objects on the stack. A spell is a card that has been cast and is on the stack. An activated or triggered ability is an ability on the stack, separate from its source. 'Counter target spell' (like Counterspell) only works on spells - cards being cast. 'Counter target activated or triggered ability' (like Stifle) only works on abilities. Some cards like Force of Negation specify noncreature spells.

instruction: Can I Pyroblast a spell that isn't blue?
output: Yes, you can cast Pyroblast targeting any spell (or permanent for its other mode), but it will only counter the spell if it's blue. Pyroblast says 'counter target spell if it's blue,' so it can legally target any spell but does nothing if the target isn't blue. This is relevant in Legacy because you can cast it to increase your storm count or to satisfy other conditions, even without a blue target.

instruction: How does Chalice of the Void's triggered ability interact with the stack?
output: Chalice of the Void's ability triggers whenever a player casts a spell with mana value equal to the number of charge counters on Chalice. The triggered ability goes on the stack above the spell that triggered it. When it resolves, it counters that spell. Players can respond to the triggered ability - for example, the spell's controller could cast another spell in response, though that too might trigger Chalice.

instruction: Can mana abilities be responded to?
output: No. Mana abilities don't use the stack and resolve immediately (rule 405.6c). A mana ability is an activated ability that could produce mana and doesn't have a target (with some exceptions). Tapping a land for mana, activating a mana dork, and similar effects all resolve instantly. This means you can't respond to your opponent tapping their lands, and you can't Stifle a basic land's mana ability.

instruction: What are the steps of the combat phase?
output: The combat phase has five steps in order: Beginning of Combat, Declare Attackers, Declare Blockers, Combat Damage, and End of Combat. The Declare Blockers and Combat Damage steps are skipped if no creatures are declared as attackers. If any attacking or blocking creature has first strike or double strike, there are two separate Combat Damage steps. Players get priority during each step after turn-based actions are completed.

instruction: When can I cast instants during combat?
output: You can cast instants during any combat step whenever you have priority. Notably, there is a Beginning of Combat step before attackers are declared where both players get priority. After attackers are declared, both players get priority again. After blockers are declared, both players get priority before damage. And after combat damage, both players get priority in the End of Combat step. Each of these is a window for instant-speed interaction.

instruction: How does first strike work in combat?
output: First strike creates an additional combat damage step. Creatures with first strike deal their combat damage in the first combat damage step, before creatures without first strike. Then there's a second combat damage step where creatures without first strike (and those with double strike again) deal their damage. If a creature is destroyed in the first damage step, it never deals its damage in the second step.

instruction: How does double strike work?
output: Double strike causes a creature to deal combat damage in both the first and second combat damage steps. Like first strike, it creates two combat damage steps. The creature with double strike deals its full power in damage during the first step (alongside first strikers), and its full power again during the second step (alongside regular creatures). This effectively doubles the creature's combat damage output.

instruction: What does Protection from a color prevent?
output: Protection prevents four things, remembered by the acronym DEBT: Damage from sources of that quality, Enchanting/Equipping by permanents of that quality, Blocking by creatures of that quality, and Targeting by spells or abilities of that quality. For example, a creature with protection from red can't be damaged by red sources, can't be enchanted by red Auras, can't be blocked by red creatures, and can't be targeted by red spells or abilities.

instruction: How does trample work with deathtouch?
output: When a creature with both trample and deathtouch attacks and is blocked, it only needs to assign 1 damage to each blocking creature (since any amount of damage from a deathtouch source is considered lethal). The rest of the damage tramples over to the defending player. For example, a 5/5 with trample and deathtouch blocked by three 4/4 creatures only needs to assign 1 damage to each blocker and can assign the remaining 2 to the defending player.

instruction: How does blocking with multiple creatures work?
output: The defending player can block an attacking creature with multiple creatures if no rules prevent it. The attacking player then orders the blocking creatures in the order they'll receive damage. When assigning combat damage, the attacker must assign lethal damage to the first creature in the order before assigning any to the next, and so on. Any remaining damage can be assigned to the last creature or, if the attacker has trample, to the defending player.

instruction: When is the correct time to activate Maze of Ith in Legacy?
output: Maze of Ith can be activated during any combat step since it says 'untap target attacking creature. Prevent all combat damage that would be dealt to and dealt by that creature this turn.' The key timing is that you can activate it after damage in the combat damage step - the creature has already dealt its damage, but Maze untaps it. This is commonly used on your own creatures like Knight of the Reliquary to get both the attack trigger/damage and have it untapped.

instruction: Can I bounce a creature with Karakas during combat in Legacy?
output: Yes. Karakas can return a target legendary creature to its owner's hand at any time you have priority, including during combat. You can bounce an attacking legendary creature after blockers are declared to remove it from combat (it deals no damage), or bounce your own legendary creature to save it from removal. Bouncing an attacking creature removes it from combat entirely.

instruction: How does combat damage assignment work with trample against a 0-toughness creature?
output: If a creature with trample is blocked by a creature with 0 toughness (which would normally be destroyed by state-based actions), all the trample damage can be assigned to the defending player. A creature with trample needs to assign lethal damage to each blocker before trampling over. A creature with 0 toughness already has lethal damage assigned (0 is lethal for 0 toughness), so all damage can go to the player.

instruction: Can I use removal on an attacker after blockers are declared to prevent damage to my blocker?
output: Yes. After blockers are declared, both players receive priority before combat damage is dealt. You can cast an instant like Swords to Plowshares to exile an attacking creature before it deals damage. Your blocking creature would survive since the attacker is no longer on the battlefield. However, your blocker is still 'blocking' and won't deal damage to the player (unless it has trample or a similar ability).

instruction: Does first strike damage happen before regular combat damage even if the first striker is blocking?
output: Yes. First strike works the same whether the creature is attacking or blocking. If a creature with first strike blocks (or is blocked), it deals its combat damage in the first combat damage step. If it kills the creature it's in combat with during that first step, the destroyed creature won't deal damage back in the second step because it's no longer on the battlefield.

instruction: What happens if I give a creature first strike after the first combat damage step?
output: If you give a creature first strike after it has already dealt combat damage in the first combat damage step, nothing special happens - it already dealt damage normally. If you give first strike to a creature that hasn't dealt damage yet (because it didn't have first strike during the first step), it won't get an extra damage step. Giving first strike after the first damage step doesn't prevent it from dealing damage in the second step.

instruction: Can a creature with protection from red block a red creature?
output: Yes. Protection prevents being BLOCKED by creatures of that quality, not blocking them. A creature with protection from red can freely block a red creature. The red creature's combat damage would be prevented by the protection, so the protecting creature would take no damage. This can be a useful defensive strategy - your protected creature blocks and survives while the attacker's damage is fully prevented.

instruction: How does lifelink interact with combat damage in Legacy?
output: Lifelink causes you to gain life equal to the damage dealt simultaneously with the damage being dealt. This is a static ability, not a triggered ability, so the life gain happens at the same time as the damage. If a creature with lifelink deals 4 combat damage, you gain 4 life as part of that damage being dealt. Multiple instances of lifelink on the same creature are redundant and don't cause you to gain extra life.

instruction: Can True-Name Nemesis be blocked in Legacy?
output: True-Name Nemesis has protection from the chosen player. Protection means it can't be blocked by creatures controlled by that player (the B in DEBT). Since in a two-player game you typically name your opponent, their creatures cannot block True-Name Nemesis. It also can't be targeted by their spells or abilities and prevents all damage from their sources, making it extremely difficult to deal with in Legacy.

instruction: What happens when Batterskull's Germ token dies in combat?
output: When the Germ token that Batterskull is equipped to has 0 toughness (after Batterskull is unattached) or is destroyed, the token goes to the graveyard and ceases to exist as a state-based action. Batterskull remains on the battlefield unattached. You can then activate Batterskull's ability to return it to your hand and recast it, creating a new Germ token. In combat, if the equipped Germ takes lethal damage, this same process occurs.

instruction: Does damage from Orcish Bowmasters kill a creature before combat damage?
output: If Orcish Bowmasters deals damage to a creature (from its enter-the-battlefield or triggered ability) before the combat damage step, state-based actions are checked before combat damage is assigned. If that damage is lethal, the creature is destroyed before it can deal combat damage. This is why the timing of Bowmasters' trigger matters - dealing damage before combat can effectively remove a blocker or attacker.

instruction: Can I sacrifice a creature to a different effect after it takes lethal combat damage but before it dies?
output: No. When combat damage is dealt, state-based actions are checked before any player receives priority. A creature with lethal damage is destroyed as a state-based action, and players can't take actions (including sacrificing creatures) between damage being dealt and state-based actions being checked. There's no window to sacrifice a creature that has received lethal combat damage.

instruction: How does combat work if I attack with a creature that has both first strike and deathtouch?
output: This is a powerful combination. Your creature deals damage in the first combat damage step (first strike). Because it has deathtouch, any amount of damage it deals is lethal. If your creature is blocked by multiple creatures, you assign at least 1 damage to each in the first strike step - the deathtouch makes that lethal, destroying them before the second combat damage step. The destroyed blockers never deal their combat damage back.

instruction: Can I activate equipment abilities during combat?
output: Equip abilities have the restriction 'Activate only as a sorcery,' meaning you can only equip during your main phase when the stack is empty and you have priority. You cannot equip during combat. However, some equipment have other activated abilities (like Batterskull's bounce ability or Umezawa's Jitte's counter-removal abilities) that can be activated at instant speed during combat if they don't have sorcery-speed restrictions.

instruction: What is mana cost versus mana value (converted mana cost)?
output: Mana cost is the actual symbols printed in the top right of a card (like {2}{U}{U} for Jace, the Mind Sculptor). Mana value (formerly called converted mana cost or CMC) is the total amount of mana in that cost represented as a single number. For Jace, the mana value is 4. Generic mana costs count their number, and each colored symbol counts as 1. Mana value is what Chalice of the Void, Spell Snare, and similar cards check.

instruction: How do alternative costs work with Force of Will and Daze?
output: Alternative costs let you pay something other than the mana cost to cast a spell. Force of Will's alternative cost is exiling a blue card from your hand and paying 1 life instead of {3}{U}{U}. Daze's alternative cost is returning an Island you control to its owner's hand instead of paying {1}{U}. Only one alternative cost can be applied to a spell. The spell's mana value doesn't change - Force of Will's mana value is still 5 even when cast for its alternative cost.

instruction: Can I pay for Daze's alternative cost if I have no Islands?
output: No. Daze's alternative cost requires you to return an Island you control to its owner's hand. If you control no Islands, you cannot pay this cost. The rule is that a player can't pay a cost without having the necessary resources to pay it fully (rule 118.3). You would need to pay the regular mana cost of {1}{U} instead. Note that dual lands like Volcanic Island and Tropical Island count as Islands for this purpose.

instruction: How does Chalice of the Void interact with spells of different mana values?
output: Chalice of the Void triggers whenever a player casts a spell with mana value equal to the number of charge counters on it. Chalice enters with X charge counters where X is the value chosen when casting it. So Chalice on 1 counters all spells with mana value 1 (Brainstorm, Swords to Plowshares, Delver of Secrets). Chalice on 0 counters mana value 0 spells like Lotus Petal and Mox Diamond. The trigger is not optional.

instruction: Does Chalice of the Void on 0 counter Lotus Petal?
output: Yes. Lotus Petal has a mana cost of {0}, giving it a mana value of 0. When Chalice of the Void has 0 charge counters, it triggers whenever a spell with mana value 0 is cast. Since Lotus Petal is cast as a spell with mana value 0, Chalice will trigger and counter it. This also applies to other 0-cost artifacts like Mox Diamond, Chrome Mox, and Lion's Eye Diamond.

instruction: What counts as a free spell in Legacy?
output: A 'free spell' generally refers to spells cast using alternative costs that don't require mana. Key examples include Force of Will (exile a blue card + 1 life), Force of Negation (exile a blue card, only on opponent's turns), Daze (return an Island), Foil (return an Island + discard an Island card), and Solitude/Endurance/Fury (exile a card of matching color). Spells with mana value 0 like Lotus Petal and Mox Diamond are also effectively 'free' since they cost {0}.

instruction: How does Chrome Mox's imprint ability work?
output: When Chrome Mox enters the battlefield, you may exile a nonartifact, nonland card from your hand. If you do, Chrome Mox gains the ability to tap for one mana of any of that card's colors. This is called 'imprinting.' If you don't exile a card, Chrome Mox can't produce colored mana. The exiled card determines what color mana Chrome Mox produces for the rest of the game. Note that imprinting is optional but Chrome Mox is useless for colored mana without it.

instruction: How does Thalia, Guardian of Thraben's tax interact with alternative costs?
output: Thalia's ability makes noncreature spells cost {1} more. This additional cost applies on top of any alternative cost being paid (rule 118.9d). So if you cast Force of Will using its alternative cost, you still need to pay {1} extra from Thalia in addition to exiling a blue card and paying 1 life. Similarly, Daze's alternative cost of returning an Island still requires paying {1} more with Thalia on the battlefield.

instruction: How does Daze math work in Legacy when my opponent has untapped lands?
output: When considering Daze, count your opponent's untapped mana sources. If they have exactly enough mana to cast their spell with nothing left over, Daze will effectively counter it (they can't pay {1}). If they have one extra untapped land, they can pay for one Daze. The Daze player bounces an Island as a cost, temporarily losing a land. Playing around Daze often means leaving extra mana open or waiting an extra turn to cast key spells.

instruction: Can additional costs be reduced by cost reduction effects?
output: Yes. All costs (mana cost, alternative costs, additional costs) are added together and then cost reduction effects are applied. For example, if Thalia adds {1} to your noncreature spell and you have a Sapphire Medallion reducing blue spells by {1}, they cancel out. Cost reductions are applied after all increases. However, cost reductions affecting generic mana can only reduce the generic portion of the total cost.

instruction: What is the mana value of a spell with X in its cost?
output: On the stack, X is the chosen value, so the mana value includes X. For example, Chalice of the Void cast with X=1 has a mana value of 2 on the stack ({X}{X} where X=1 means 1+1=2). In all other zones (hand, graveyard, library), X is treated as 0. So Chalice of the Void in your hand has a mana value of 0. This distinction matters for cards like Spell Snare or Flusterstorm targeting spells on the stack.

instruction: Can I tap a land for mana in response to Wasteland targeting it?
output: Yes. When your opponent activates Wasteland targeting your nonbasic land, the ability goes on the stack. You receive priority and can tap your targeted land for mana in response. The mana enters your mana pool, and then Wasteland's ability resolves and destroys your land. You still have the mana, but it will empty at the end of the current step or phase. This is why you should tap the targeted land for mana before it's destroyed.

instruction: How does Delver of Secrets' mana value interact with Spell Pierce?
output: Delver of Secrets is a creature spell with mana cost {U}, giving it a mana value of 1. Spell Pierce only targets noncreature spells, so it cannot counter Delver of Secrets. However, once transformed into Insectile Aberration, the mana value is still based on the front face's mana cost (1). Mana value doesn't change when a card transforms. Cards like Abrupt Decay that care about mana value 3 or less can target the transformed creature.

instruction: What happens if I cast a spell and my opponent activates Thalia's ability after I've already paid costs?
output: Thalia, Guardian of Thraben's cost increase is a static ability that applies as you're casting a spell. It's not an activated ability your opponent can use in response. If Thalia is on the battlefield when you start casting a spell, you must pay the extra {1}. Your opponent can't 'activate' Thalia to increase the cost after you've already started casting - the timing of static abilities is determined by whether the permanent is on the battlefield when costs are calculated.

instruction: How does Lion's Eye Diamond work with Infernal Tutor in Legacy?
output: Lion's Eye Diamond can only be activated as an instant while you have priority, not during spell casting. The key combo is: cast Infernal Tutor (which checks 'hellbent' - no cards in hand - on resolution), then hold priority and crack LED, discarding your hand for 3 mana. When Infernal Tutor resolves, you have no cards in hand (hellbent), so you can search for any card. You then use the LED mana to cast what you find.

instruction: What is the difference between paying mana costs and paying total costs?
output: The mana cost is printed on the card (like {1}{U} for Daze). The total cost to cast a spell starts with the mana cost (or an alternative cost), plus any additional costs (kicker, Thalia's tax, etc.), minus any cost reductions. For example, casting Brainstorm ({U}) with Thalia on the battlefield costs {1}{U} total. The mana value is always based on the printed mana cost, regardless of what was actually paid.

instruction: Can I use Cavern of Souls to make a creature uncounterable even with Chalice on the stack?
output: Yes. Cavern of Souls says the creature spell 'can't be countered.' Chalice of the Void's triggered ability goes on the stack and targets the spell, but when it tries to resolve and counter the spell, it fails because the spell can't be countered. The Chalice trigger still resolves (it's not countered itself), but it has no effect. This is relevant in Legacy for decks like Eldrazi or Goblins that use Cavern to push creatures through Chalice.

instruction: How does the mana value of split cards work (like Fire // Ice)?
output: In all zones except the stack, a split card has two sets of characteristics and its mana value is the combined mana value of both halves. Fire // Ice has a combined mana value of 4 (Fire is 2, Ice is 2). On the stack, only the half being cast matters - casting Fire has mana value 2, casting Ice has mana value 2. This means Chalice of the Void on 2 would counter either half when cast.

instruction: What is a permanent versus a spell in MTG?
output: A spell is a card on the stack that has been cast (or a copy of a card on the stack). Instants and sorceries are spells while on the stack and go to the graveyard after resolving. A permanent is a card or token on the battlefield. Creatures, artifacts, enchantments, lands, planeswalkers, and battles are permanents once they're on the battlefield. A creature card in your hand is neither a spell nor a permanent - it's just a card. It becomes a spell when cast and a permanent when it resolves and enters the battlefield.

instruction: When does a creature become a permanent?
output: A creature card becomes a permanent when it enters the battlefield, which happens as the spell resolves. While the creature spell is on the stack, it is a spell, not a permanent. Once it resolves and moves from the stack to the battlefield, it becomes a creature permanent. This distinction matters because effects that target 'creatures' on the battlefield can't target creature spells on the stack, and counterspells that target spells can't target creatures on the battlefield.

instruction: What zones exist in Magic and how do cards move between them?
output: There are seven main zones: Library, Hand, Battlefield, Graveyard, Stack, Exile, and Command. Cards move between zones through game actions: drawing moves cards from library to hand, casting moves from hand to stack, resolving moves from stack to battlefield (or graveyard for instants/sorceries). Dying moves from battlefield to graveyard. When a card changes zones, it becomes a new object with no memory of its previous existence, with a few specific exceptions listed in the rules.

instruction: How does the graveyard work as a zone?
output: The graveyard is a public zone where each player has their own graveyard pile. Cards go there when discarded, destroyed, sacrificed, milled, or when instants/sorceries finish resolving. The order of cards in the graveyard matters in some formats and can't be rearranged (rule 400.5). Cards in the graveyard can be targeted by effects like Surgical Extraction or Reanimate. Some cards have abilities that function from the graveyard, like flashback or Dread Return.

instruction: How does the exile zone work?
output: The exile zone is a shared public zone. Cards are exiled by various effects like Swords to Plowshares, Rest in Peace, or Force of Will's alternative cost. Exiled cards are generally considered removed from the game for most practical purposes, though some effects specifically interact with exiled cards (like Karn, the Great Creator retrieving artifacts). Face-down exiled cards can only be looked at by players permitted by the effect that exiled them.

instruction: What is the library manipulation rule in MTG?
output: A library must be kept as a single face-down pile, and players can't look at or change the order of cards in it (rule 401.2). However, effects may allow searching, revealing, or rearranging cards in a library. When an effect puts multiple cards in specific positions in a library simultaneously, the owner arranges them in any order without revealing that order. Any player may count the number of cards in any player's library at any time.

instruction: How does Surgical Extraction timing work in Legacy?
output: Surgical Extraction can be cast any time you have priority because it has a Phyrexian mana cost (pay 2 life or {B}). The key timing is waiting until the target card is in the graveyard. For example, after a fetchland resolves and goes to the graveyard, you can Surgical it. After your opponent casts Entomb and it resolves putting a creature in the graveyard, you can Surgical that creature. You can also Surgical in response to a Reanimate targeting a creature in the graveyard.

instruction: How does Entomb work and when can I interact with the card it finds?
output: Entomb is an instant that searches your library for a card and puts it into your graveyard. The search and placement happen during resolution. No player has priority during resolution, so you can't interact with the card until after Entomb finishes resolving. Once it resolves and priority is passed, you can then target the card with Surgical Extraction or other graveyard interaction. This is why Entomb into Reanimate gives opponents a window to respond between the two spells.

instruction: Can a token exist in a zone other than the battlefield?
output: Tokens can exist briefly in other zones. If a token leaves the battlefield, it goes to the appropriate zone (graveyard, exile, hand, library) and then ceases to exist as a state-based action the next time they are checked. This means tokens do trigger 'when this dies' abilities because they momentarily exist in the graveyard. However, tokens can never exist in a zone other than the battlefield for more than a brief moment.

instruction: What happens to Auras when the enchanted permanent leaves the battlefield?
output: When the permanent an Aura is enchanting leaves the battlefield, the Aura is put into its owner's graveyard as a state-based action. The Aura isn't destroyed - it's simply moved to the graveyard because it's no longer attached to a legal permanent. This means the Aura won't trigger 'when destroyed' abilities but will trigger 'when put into the graveyard from the battlefield' abilities.

instruction: Does a card that changes zones remember what happened to it before?
output: No. An object that moves from one zone to another becomes a new object with no memory of its previous existence (rule 400.7). For example, if a creature is exiled and then returned to the battlefield, it's a completely new permanent - it won't remember any counters, Auras, damage, or effects that applied to it before. There are specific exceptions, like effects that track a card as it moves to a public zone, but the general rule is that zone changes reset an object's identity.

instruction: Can I target a card in my opponent's graveyard with a spell?
output: Yes, if the spell says it targets a card in a graveyard. The graveyard is a public zone, so you can see all cards in it. Cards like Reanimate ('target creature card in a graveyard'), Surgical Extraction ('target card in a graveyard'), and Endurance (shuffling a graveyard into its owner's library) can all interact with your opponent's graveyard. You can also target cards in your own graveyard with these effects.

instruction: How do replacement effects work with cards entering the battlefield?
output: Replacement effects modify how an event happens. If a card says 'enters the battlefield tapped' or 'enters with X counters,' these are replacement effects that modify the enter-the-battlefield event. They don't trigger after entering - they change how the entry happens. For example, Rest in Peace has a replacement effect: 'If a card or token would be put into a graveyard from anywhere, exile it instead.' The card never reaches the graveyard at all.

instruction: What happens if two replacement effects try to modify the same event?
output: If multiple replacement effects try to modify the same event, the affected player (or controller of the affected object) chooses which one to apply first. After applying one replacement effect, the event may have changed such that the other no longer applies, or it may still apply. Each replacement effect can only apply to a given event once. This ordering can be strategically important, such as choosing the order of replacement effects when a creature would enter the battlefield.

instruction: Can I Reanimate a creature in response to my opponent Surgically Extracting it?
output: Yes. If your opponent targets a creature card in your graveyard with Surgical Extraction, you receive priority and can respond by casting Reanimate targeting that same creature. Your Reanimate resolves first (LIFO), putting the creature onto the battlefield. When Surgical Extraction tries to resolve, its target is no longer in the graveyard and is countered by game rules. Your opponent still gets to search for other copies, though.

instruction: What is the command zone used for in Legacy?
output: In Legacy (and most constructed formats), the command zone is primarily used for emblems created by planeswalker abilities. Emblems exist in the command zone and can't be interacted with by spells or abilities - they just apply their effects for the rest of the game. In Commander format, commanders also use the command zone, but in standard Legacy, the command zone mainly holds emblems and occasionally companions declared at the start of the game.

instruction: How does Flash (the keyword ability) work?
output: Flash is a static ability that allows you to cast the card any time you could cast an instant (rule 702.8a). This means you can cast creatures, enchantments, or artifacts with flash during your opponent's turn, in response to spells or abilities, or during combat. The spell still goes on the stack and can be responded to normally. Flash is extremely powerful in Legacy - cards like Teferi, Time Raveler can grant flash to your spells or restrict opponents from casting at instant speed.

instruction: How does flying work as an evasion ability?
output: Flying is an evasion ability that restricts how a creature can be blocked. A creature with flying can only be blocked by creatures with flying or reach. A creature with flying can block both flying and non-flying creatures normally. Multiple instances of flying on the same creature are redundant. In Legacy, flying is relevant on threats like Delver of Secrets (once flipped to Insectile Aberration) and Baleful Strix.

instruction: How does vigilance work?
output: Vigilance is a static ability that modifies the declare attackers step. Normally, attacking causes a creature to tap. A creature with vigilance doesn't tap when it attacks, meaning it can attack and still be available to block during your opponent's turn, activate tap abilities, or be tapped for other purposes. Multiple instances of vigilance are redundant.

instruction: How does haste work?
output: Haste allows a creature to attack and use tap/untap abilities the turn it enters the battlefield. Normally, a creature can't attack or use abilities with the tap or untap symbol in their cost unless you've controlled it continuously since the beginning of your most recent turn (informally called 'summoning sickness'). Haste bypasses this restriction entirely. In Legacy, haste is critical on threats like Monastery Swiftspear and Goblin Lackey.

instruction: How does deathtouch work?
output: Deathtouch is a static ability. Any creature with toughness greater than 0 that has been dealt damage by a source with deathtouch is destroyed as a state-based action. Additionally, any nonzero amount of combat damage assigned by a source with deathtouch is considered lethal for assigning combat damage. This means a 1/1 deathtouch creature only needs to assign 1 damage to kill any creature. Deathtouch works from any zone, not just the battlefield.

instruction: How does lifelink work?
output: Lifelink is a static ability. Damage dealt by a source with lifelink causes that source's controller to gain that much life simultaneously with the damage being dealt. Because it's a static ability (not a triggered ability), the life gain happens at the same time as the damage - there's no trigger to respond to. If a creature has multiple instances of lifelink, they're redundant and you only gain life once.

instruction: How does the Storm mechanic count spells?
output: Storm is a triggered ability that triggers when you cast a spell with storm. It counts every other spell cast before it during the current turn by all players - not just you. The storm trigger then creates a copy of the spell for each spell counted. These copies are put directly on the stack and are not themselves 'cast,' so they don't count for other storm spells. In Legacy, Tendrils of Agony with a storm count of 9 creates 9 copies for 10 total instances.

instruction: How does the Cascade mechanic work?
output: Cascade is a triggered ability that triggers when you cast a spell with cascade. You exile cards from the top of your library one at a time until you exile a nonland card with mana value less than the spell with cascade. You may cast that card without paying its mana cost. All other exiled cards go to the bottom of your library in a random order. Cascade was significant in Legacy with cards like Shardless Agent cascading into Ancestral Vision or Bloodbraid Elf cascading into impactful 3-drops.

instruction: How does Dredge work as a replacement effect?
output: Dredge is a static ability that functions only while the card is in your graveyard. 'Dredge N' means: if you would draw a card, you may instead mill N cards and return the card with dredge to your hand. This is a replacement effect that replaces your draw. You must have at least N cards in your library to use it. In Legacy Dredge, players replace nearly every draw with dredge effects from cards like Golgari Grave-Troll (dredge 6) and Stinkweed Imp (dredge 5) to fill their graveyard.

instruction: How does Annihilator work with Emrakul, the Aeons Torn?
output: Annihilator is a triggered ability that triggers whenever the creature attacks. 'Annihilator 6' on Emrakul means: whenever Emrakul attacks, the defending player must sacrifice 6 permanents. This trigger goes on the stack after attackers are declared. The defending player can respond to it but must sacrifice the permanents when it resolves (they choose which ones). The sacrifices happen before blockers are declared, often leaving the opponent with nothing to block with.

instruction: How does Infect work with poison counters?
output: Infect modifies how a source deals damage. Damage dealt to a player by a source with infect causes that player to get that many poison counters instead of losing life. Damage dealt to creatures by a source with infect is dealt as -1/-1 counters instead of regular damage. A player with 10 or more poison counters loses the game as a state-based action. In Legacy, Infect decks use Glistener Elf and Blighted Agent alongside pump spells to quickly reach 10 poison.

instruction: How does Force of Will's pitch cost work mechanically?
output: Force of Will has an alternative cost: you may exile a blue card from your hand and pay 1 life rather than paying its mana cost of {3}{U}{U}. Exiling the blue card and paying life are both part of the cost, done simultaneously during casting. The exiled card must be blue (have blue in its color identity from its mana cost or color indicator). The spell's mana value remains 5 regardless of which cost was paid. This alternative cost can be paid on any player's turn.

instruction: Can Stifle counter a triggered ability from entering the battlefield?
output: Yes. Stifle counters a target activated or triggered ability. Enter-the-battlefield (ETB) triggered abilities use the stack like any other triggered ability. When a permanent enters the battlefield and triggers an ability, that trigger goes on the stack and can be targeted by Stifle. In Legacy, Stifle commonly counters fetchland abilities, Stoneforge Mystic's ETB, Show and Tell's triggers, and similar effects.

instruction: How does hexproof differ from shroud?
output: Hexproof means the permanent can't be targeted by spells or abilities your opponents control - but you can still target it yourself. Shroud means the permanent can't be targeted by any spells or abilities at all, including your own. In Legacy, True-Name Nemesis has protection (even stronger than hexproof) while older cards like Invisible Stalker have hexproof. Shroud appears on older cards but has been largely replaced by hexproof in modern design.

instruction: How does the Flashback ability work?
output: Flashback is an ability that lets you cast a spell from your graveyard by paying its flashback cost instead of its mana cost. You can only use flashback at the time you could normally cast the spell (sorcery speed for sorceries, instant speed for instants). When the spell resolves or leaves the stack for any reason, it is exiled instead of going anywhere else. Flashback costs are alternative costs. In Legacy, Cabal Therapy's flashback of sacrificing a creature is a key combo piece.

instruction: What does indestructible mean and what can still remove an indestructible permanent?
output: Indestructible means the permanent can't be destroyed by effects that say 'destroy' or by lethal damage. However, indestructible doesn't protect against sacrifice effects, exile effects, effects that reduce toughness to 0 or less, being bounced to hand, or being countered as a spell on the stack. In Legacy, Swords to Plowshares (exile) and Council's Judgment (sacrifice-like effect) both deal with indestructible permanents effectively.

instruction: How does the Miracle mechanic work?
output: Miracle is an ability that triggers when you draw a card and it's the first card you've drawn this turn. You must reveal it as you draw it. When the miracle trigger resolves, you may cast the card for its miracle cost. If you don't cast it, it goes to your hand normally. The timing is critical: you must reveal immediately upon drawing, before looking at any other cards. In Legacy, Terminus for its miracle cost of {W} is a key sweeper in Miracles/Control decks.

instruction: How does Phyrexian mana work?
output: Phyrexian mana symbols can be paid with either one mana of the appropriate color or by paying 2 life. For example, {U/P} can be paid with {U} or 2 life. This means spells with Phyrexian mana can be cast without any mana by paying life. The mana value counts Phyrexian symbols as 1 each regardless of how they were paid. In Legacy, Gitaxian Probe ({U/P}) and Surgical Extraction ({B/P}) are commonly cast by paying life.

instruction: Can I Wasteland my own land?
output: Yes. Wasteland says 'Destroy target nonbasic land.' It doesn't say 'target nonbasic land an opponent controls,' so you can legally target your own nonbasic lands. While this seems counterproductive, there are strategic reasons to do this in Legacy, such as destroying your own Dark Depths to trigger the Marit Lage token creation, or sacrificing a land to deny your opponent Surgical Extraction information.

instruction: Can Show and Tell put an enchantment into play?
output: Yes. Show and Tell says each player may put an artifact, creature, enchantment, or land card from their hand onto the battlefield. Enchantments are explicitly listed. This is relevant in Legacy because Show and Tell decks sometimes put Omniscience into play (an enchantment that lets you cast spells without paying mana costs), or opponents can put in Auras like Karakas or other problematic permanents to answer the Show and Tell player's threat.

instruction: Does Karakas bounce your own creature? Is this useful?
output: Yes. Karakas says 'Return target legendary creature to its owner's hand,' and you can target your own legendary creatures. This is commonly used in Legacy to protect your own legendary creatures from removal by bouncing them in response, to re-trigger enter-the-battlefield abilities on your own creatures, or to save your legendary creature from combat. It also bounces opposing legendary creatures like Emrakul or Griselbrand put in via Show and Tell.

instruction: How does Thespian's Stage plus Dark Depths work?
output: Thespian's Stage can copy Dark Depths by paying {2} and tapping. When it becomes a copy of Dark Depths, it doesn't enter the battlefield (it's already there), so it doesn't get ice counters. State-based actions then check and see a Dark Depths with no ice counters on it. The legendary rule also applies - you sacrifice one of the two Dark Depths (the original). Then the copy with no ice counters triggers, sacrificing itself to create Marit Lage, a legendary 20/20 indestructible flying black Avatar token.

instruction: How does Painter's Servant plus Grindstone work?
output: Painter's Servant makes all cards in all zones the chosen color in addition to their other colors. Grindstone mills the top two cards of target player's library and checks if they share a color. With Painter's Servant active, every card shares the chosen color, so Grindstone's condition is always met, and it repeats, milling two more cards, over and over until the entire library is gone. This is a two-card combo that mills the opponent's entire library.

instruction: Can I Brainstorm in response to Thoughtseize?
output: Yes. When your opponent casts Thoughtseize targeting you, it goes on the stack and you receive priority. You can cast Brainstorm in response. Brainstorm resolves first, letting you draw three cards and put two back on top of your library. Then Thoughtseize resolves and your opponent looks at your hand - but you've hidden your two best cards on top of your library where Thoughtseize can't reach them. This is one of the most important plays in Legacy.

instruction: Does Orcish Bowmasters trigger off Brainstorm?
output: Yes. Orcish Bowmasters triggers each time an opponent draws a card, and Brainstorm draws three cards one at a time. Each individual draw triggers Bowmasters separately, resulting in three triggers. Each trigger independently creates a 1/1 Orc Army token or puts a +1/+1 counter on one and deals 1 damage to any target. This interaction makes Brainstorm much riskier against Bowmasters and has significantly impacted Legacy's metagame.

instruction: How does Dread Return work from the graveyard?
output: Dread Return has a flashback cost of sacrificing three creatures instead of paying mana. This means you can cast it from your graveyard during your main phase by sacrificing three creatures you control. It targets a creature card in a graveyard and puts it directly onto the battlefield. In Legacy Dredge, the deck creates multiple Zombie tokens via Bridge from Below and Narcomoeba triggers, then sacrifices three of them to flashback Dread Return, reanimating a powerful creature.

instruction: What is the difference between Force of Negation and Force of Will?
output: Both can be cast for free by exiling a blue card from your hand. Force of Will can be cast on any turn (yours or your opponent's) for its alternative cost and counters any spell. Force of Negation's alternative cost (exile a blue card, no life payment) can only be used during an opponent's turn, and it only counters noncreature spells. On your own turn, Force of Negation must be cast for {1}{U}{U}. Force of Will also requires 1 life payment with its alternative cost; Force of Negation does not.

instruction: Can I use Swords to Plowshares in response to a creature entering the battlefield?
output: Yes. When a creature enters the battlefield, if it has any enter-the-battlefield triggers, those go on the stack. You receive priority and can cast Swords to Plowshares targeting that creature. The creature will be exiled before the ETB trigger resolves (though the trigger will still resolve - removing the source doesn't remove the triggered ability from the stack). Even if the creature has no ETB trigger, you can target it once any player would receive priority.

instruction: How does Natural Order work in Legacy?
output: Natural Order costs {2}{G}{G} and requires you to sacrifice a green creature as an additional cost. It then lets you search your library for a green creature card and put it directly onto the battlefield. The sacrifice is a cost (paid when casting), so it can't be responded to by targeting the creature to be sacrificed. In Legacy, this typically involves sacrificing a small green creature to put Progenitus or another powerful green creature into play.

instruction: How does Sneak Attack work in Legacy?
output: Sneak Attack is an enchantment with the activated ability '{R}: You may put a creature card from your hand onto the battlefield. That creature gains haste. Sacrifice the creature at the beginning of the next end step.' This lets you cheat expensive creatures like Emrakul or Griselbrand into play for just {R}. The creature can attack immediately (it has haste) but is sacrificed at end of turn. Multiple activations can put multiple creatures in during the same turn.

instruction: Can Chalice of the Void counter itself?
output: No. Chalice of the Void's triggered ability only functions on the battlefield ('Whenever a player casts a spell...'). While Chalice is on the stack being cast, it's a spell and its triggered ability isn't active yet. However, if there's already a Chalice on the battlefield with charge counters equal to the mana value of the new Chalice spell, the existing Chalice will trigger and counter the new one.

instruction: How does Aether Vial work in Legacy Death and Taxes?
output: Aether Vial enters the battlefield and gets a charge counter added at your upkeep. You can tap it to put a creature card from your hand with mana value equal to its charge counters directly onto the battlefield. This doesn't count as casting a spell, so it can't be countered by normal counterspells or Chalice of the Void. It also doesn't require mana, letting you deploy creatures while leaving mana open for Rishadan Port or Wasteland activations.

instruction: Can Green Sun's Zenith find Dryad Arbor when X equals 0?
output: Yes. Green Sun's Zenith searches for a green creature card with mana value X or less. Dryad Arbor is a green creature land with no mana cost, giving it a mana value of 0. When X=0, you pay just {G} for Green Sun's Zenith and can find Dryad Arbor. This is a common turn-1 play in Legacy, effectively making Green Sun's Zenith a {G} ramp spell that also thins your deck.

instruction: How does Stoneforge Mystic work to put equipment into play?
output: Stoneforge Mystic has two abilities. First, when it enters the battlefield, you search your library for an Equipment card and put it into your hand. Second, you can pay {1}{W} and tap Stoneforge to put an Equipment card from your hand directly onto the battlefield. This second ability bypasses casting, so the Equipment can't be countered. In Legacy, this commonly puts Batterskull or Kaldra Compleat into play as early as turn 3.

instruction: What happens if my opponent casts Abrupt Decay targeting my permanent? Can I counter it?
output: No. Abrupt Decay says 'This spell can't be countered.' This means counterspells like Force of Will targeting Abrupt Decay will resolve but fail to counter it. However, you can still respond in other ways - you can bounce your permanent to save it, give it hexproof, sacrifice it for value, or make it an illegal target. You can also use Stifle if Abrupt Decay somehow had a triggered ability, but the spell itself is uncounterable.

instruction: Does Rest in Peace stop Dredge decks entirely?
output: Rest in Peace is extremely effective against Dredge. It has a replacement effect that exiles cards that would go to any graveyard. This means cards that are milled, discarded, or destroyed go to exile instead of the graveyard. Dredge requires cards to be in the graveyard to function - dredge abilities only work from the graveyard, Bridge from Below needs to be in the graveyard, and Narcomoeba triggers from milling. With Rest in Peace active, the entire Dredge engine is shut down.

instruction: How does Terminus interact with indestructible creatures in Legacy?
output: Terminus puts all creatures on the bottom of their owners' libraries. This is not 'destroying' them, so indestructible doesn't help. It also doesn't target, so hexproof, shroud, and protection don't help either. The creatures aren't dying (they're changing zones to the library, not the graveyard), so death triggers don't fire and Bridge from Below doesn't trigger. This makes Terminus one of the most effective sweepers in Legacy.

instruction: Can I activate Wasteland targeting a basic land?
output: No. Wasteland says 'Destroy target nonbasic land.' Basic lands (Plains, Island, Swamp, Mountain, Forest) are not legal targets. However, many lands that produce basic land types are nonbasic - dual lands like Underground Sea, shock lands, and other nonbasic lands are all legal targets. Snow-covered basics (Snow-Covered Island, etc.) are basic lands and cannot be targeted by Wasteland.

instruction: How does Life from the Loam work with fetchlands in Legacy Lands?
output: Life from the Loam returns up to three land cards from your graveyard to your hand. Combined with fetchlands, this creates a powerful engine: you sacrifice fetchlands to search for lands (putting the fetchlands in the graveyard), then cast Life from the Loam to return the fetchlands to your hand, and replay them. Life from the Loam also has dredge 3, so you can dredge it back instead of drawing, milling more lands into your graveyard to recur.

instruction: Can I imprint a land card on Chrome Mox?
output: No. Chrome Mox's imprint ability says 'you may exile a nonartifact, nonland card from your hand.' Land cards are explicitly excluded, as are artifact cards. This means you need to exile a creature, enchantment, instant, sorcery, or planeswalker card. If all you have in hand are lands and artifacts, you won't be able to imprint anything and Chrome Mox will only produce colorless mana.

instruction: How does Rishadan Port work in Legacy Death and Taxes?
output: Rishadan Port has the ability '{1}, {T}: Tap target land.' You can activate this during your opponent's upkeep to tap one of their lands before they can use it for mana, effectively denying them a mana source for the turn. Since Port requires {1} and tapping itself, it slows down your own mana development too, but combined with Aether Vial (which deploys creatures without mana), the tax on your opponent is much more significant than the cost to you.

instruction: How does the interaction between Flickerwisp and an opponent's permanent work?
output: Flickerwisp exiles target permanent when it enters the battlefield. The exiled permanent returns at the beginning of the next end step under its owner's control. If you exile an opponent's land during their upkeep (via Aether Vial), they lose access to it for most of their turn. If you exile an opponent's token, it ceases to exist in exile and never returns. You can also exile your own permanents to re-trigger ETB abilities.

instruction: What happens when you Reanimate Griselbrand in Legacy?
output: Reanimate costs {B} and puts a target creature card from any graveyard onto the battlefield, then you lose life equal to its mana value. Griselbrand has mana value 8, so you lose 8 life (going from 20 to 12). Griselbrand then has the ability to pay 7 life to draw 7 cards. With 12 life remaining, you can immediately activate this once, drawing 7 cards (going to 5 life), and potentially find more ways to win or protect Griselbrand.

instruction: Can Plague Engineer shut down an opponent's tribal deck in Legacy?
output: Yes. Plague Engineer gives all creatures of the chosen type your opponents control -1/-1. If you name the relevant creature type (Human against Humans, Goblin against Goblins, Elf against Elves), all of your opponent's 1-toughness creatures of that type will die immediately as state-based actions. Larger creatures are weakened. This is a powerful sideboard card in Legacy that can devastate tribal strategies.

instruction: How does Omniscience work when put into play off Show and Tell?
output: Omniscience is an enchantment that says you may cast spells from your hand without paying their mana costs. When put into play via Show and Tell, you immediately have Omniscience on the battlefield. On your next priority, you can cast any spells from your hand for free. Typically, the Show and Tell player will cast a card draw spell to find more action, then chain free spells together, often winning that turn with cards like Cunning Wish into a win condition.

instruction: How does the legendary rule work with multiple copies?
output: The 'legend rule' is a state-based action. If a player controls two or more legendary permanents with the same name, that player chooses one and puts the rest into their owners' graveyards. This only affects permanents controlled by the same player - you and your opponent can each control a legendary permanent with the same name. This is why Karakas is so powerful: it can bounce opposing legendary creatures but the legend rule also helps interact with Dark Depths combos.

instruction: Can I use Crop Rotation in response to Wasteland?
output: Yes. Crop Rotation is an instant that says 'As an additional cost, sacrifice a land. Search your library for a land card and put it onto the battlefield.' When your opponent activates Wasteland targeting your land, you can respond with Crop Rotation, sacrificing the targeted land as a cost. Wasteland's ability now has no legal target (the land is gone) and is countered by game rules. Meanwhile, you get any land from your library onto the battlefield.

instruction: How does Leovold, Emissary of Trest interact with Brainstorm?
output: Leovold says each opponent can't draw more than one card per turn. Brainstorm instructs you to draw three cards. If your opponent controls Leovold, the first draw happens normally, but the second and third draws are replaced by nothing - they simply don't happen. You only draw one card, then put two cards from your hand on top of your library. This makes Leovold devastating against cantrip-heavy Legacy decks.

instruction: What is the interaction between Narset, Parter of Veils and a wheel effect?
output: Narset's static ability says each opponent can't draw more than one card each turn. If you cast a wheel effect (like Echo of Eons) while Narset is on the battlefield, each player discards their hand, then each player draws seven cards - except opponents only draw one due to Narset. You draw seven fresh cards while each opponent gets only one. This is a devastating combo that has made Narset a staple in Legacy combo and control.

instruction: How does Teferi, Time Raveler affect Legacy gameplay?
output: Teferi's static ability says each opponent can only cast spells any time they could cast a sorcery. This means opponents can't cast instants or use flash during your turn, during combat, or in response to your spells. Force of Will can't be cast for its alternative cost on your turn. Counterspells can't be used in response to anything. This essentially makes all your spells uncounterable on your turn and shuts down instant-speed interaction from your opponents.

instruction: Can you use Endurance in response to a Reanimate targeting a creature in a graveyard?
output: Yes. When your opponent casts Reanimate targeting a creature in their graveyard, you can cast Endurance (using its evoke cost by exiling a green card) in response. Endurance puts target player's graveyard on the bottom of their library in a random order. When Reanimate tries to resolve, its target is no longer in the graveyard, so it's countered by game rules. Your opponent still loses life equal to the creature's mana value since that's part of the resolution that can't happen.

instruction: How does Karn, the Great Creator's static ability affect Legacy?
output: Karn's static ability says activated abilities of artifacts your opponents control can't be activated. This shuts down Lion's Eye Diamond, Aether Vial, Lotus Petal's sacrifice ability, equipment abilities, and more. It doesn't affect triggered or static abilities, and it doesn't affect mana abilities of artifacts your opponents control (correction: it does affect mana abilities too since it says 'activated abilities'). This makes Karn devastating against artifact-heavy strategies.

instruction: What is the interaction between Aluren and creatures with mana value 3 or less?
output: Aluren is an enchantment that says any player may cast creature spells with mana value 3 or less without paying their mana costs and as though they had flash. This means you can cast small creatures at instant speed for free. In the Legacy Aluren combo, you repeatedly cast and bounce creatures like Cavern Harpy (which returns a creature to your hand when it enters) to generate infinite enter-the-battlefield triggers, typically winning with Parasitic Strix draining the opponent's life.

instruction: How does Sneak Attack interact with Emrakul, the Aeons Torn?
output: Sneak Attack puts Emrakul onto the battlefield with haste for {R}. When Emrakul attacks, its Annihilator 6 trigger forces the opponent to sacrifice 6 permanents. Emrakul also has an extra turn trigger when cast, but since Sneak Attack puts it onto the battlefield (not casting), you don't get the extra turn. Emrakul also has protection from colored spells, but can still be sacrificed to Sneak Attack's delayed trigger at end of turn. Taking 15 damage plus losing 6 permanents is usually game-ending.

instruction: Can Council's Judgment exile a True-Name Nemesis?
output: Yes. Council's Judgment doesn't target - it uses the 'will of the council' mechanic where each player votes for a nonland permanent they don't control. The permanent with the most votes (or tied for most) is exiled. Since True-Name Nemesis has protection from the chosen player, it can't be targeted, but Council's Judgment doesn't target. This makes Council's Judgment one of the few clean answers to True-Name Nemesis in Legacy.

instruction: How does Thoughtseize work and what can't it take?
output: Thoughtseize is a sorcery that costs {B}. You look at the target player's hand and choose a nonland card from it. That player discards that card, and you lose 2 life. Thoughtseize can take any nonland card: creatures, instants, sorceries, artifacts, enchantments, and planeswalkers. The only restriction is that it can't take land cards. In Legacy, Thoughtseize is a premium disruption spell that can remove combo pieces, counterspells, or threats before they're cast.

instruction: What is the Dark Ritual into Hypnotic Specter interaction? Why was this historically significant?
output: Dark Ritual adds {B}{B}{B} to your mana pool for {B}. On turn 1, you could play a Swamp, cast Dark Ritual (3 black mana), and cast Hypnotic Specter (a 2/2 flyer that forces a random discard when it deals damage). This turn-1 play was historically one of the most powerful openings in Magic. While Hypnotic Specter isn't Legacy-playable now, Dark Ritual remains a Legacy staple enabling powerful turn-1 plays in Storm and Reanimator decks.

instruction: How does Umezawa's Jitte work in combat?
output: Umezawa's Jitte gets two charge counters whenever the equipped creature deals combat damage. You can remove counters for three effects: remove 1 to give a creature +2/+2 until end of turn, remove 1 to give a creature -1/-1 until end of turn, or remove 1 to gain 2 life. The counters stay on Jitte even if the equipped creature dies, and you can activate multiple times at instant speed. In Legacy, Jitte can dominate creature matchups by repeatedly shrinking opposing creatures or pumping your own.

instruction: Can I sacrifice a land to Daze's cost and still pay for a spell that same turn?
output: Daze doesn't sacrifice a land - it returns an Island you control to your hand. This means you lose access to that land for the turn (you've already played a land for the turn typically), effectively costing you a mana. If you bounced a tapped Island, you lose nothing immediately but lose a mana next turn. If you bounced an untapped Island, you lose that mana source. Planning around this tempo loss is key to using Daze effectively in Legacy.

instruction: How does Predict work in Legacy Miracles?
output: Predict costs {1}{U} and has you name a card, then mill the top card of a target player's library. If the milled card matches the name, you draw two cards. In Legacy Miracles builds, you can use Predict on yourself after setting up the top of your library with Brainstorm or Ponder. You can also 'predict' an opponent's card if you know what's on top. If a Terminus is on top of your library, you can Predict naming Terminus, mill it, and draw two cards.

instruction: How does Monastery Swiftspear's prowess work in Legacy Burn?
output: Prowess triggers whenever you cast a noncreature spell, giving the creature +1/+1 until end of turn. Each noncreature spell triggers prowess separately. In Legacy Burn, you can chain multiple burn spells in one turn: cast Lightning Bolt (prowess triggers, Swiftspear becomes 2/3), attack, then cast another Bolt (prowess triggers again, making it 3/4 during combat). The triggers stack, so casting three noncreature spells gives +3/+3 total.

instruction: What is the difference between sacrificing and destroying a permanent?
output: Destroying a permanent is an effect that puts it into the graveyard but can be prevented by indestructible or regeneration. Sacrificing is a cost or effect where the controller puts their own permanent into the graveyard - it can't be prevented by indestructible, regeneration, or protection. This distinction matters in Legacy: Diabolic Edict forces a player to sacrifice (gets around indestructible and hexproof), while Abrupt Decay destroys (stopped by indestructible).

instruction: How does Back to Basics work against Legacy manabases?
output: Back to Basics is an enchantment that says nonbasic lands don't untap during their controller's untap step. Since most Legacy decks run very few basic lands (relying on dual lands, fetchlands, and utility lands), Back to Basics can lock an opponent out of most of their mana. It doesn't tap the lands - they just don't untap if they become tapped. Lands that were untapped when Back to Basics resolves remain untapped until used.

instruction: How does Chalice of the Void interact with Force of Will's alternative cost?
output: Chalice of the Void checks the mana value of the spell, not what was actually paid. Force of Will's mana value is always 5 (its mana cost is {3}{U}{U}), regardless of whether it was cast using its alternative cost. So Chalice with 5 counters would counter Force of Will, but Chalice with 0 counters would not. The alternative cost doesn't change the mana value of the spell.

instruction: How does Mindbreak Trap exile copies from Storm?
output: Mindbreak Trap has a 'trap' alternative cost - if an opponent cast three or more spells this turn, you may pay {0} instead of {2}{U}{U}. It exiles any number of target spells. Against Storm, when your opponent casts Tendrils of Agony and the storm trigger creates copies, those copies are on the stack as spells that can be targeted. Mindbreak Trap can exile the original and all copies. Notably, it exiles rather than counters, so it works against spells that 'can't be countered.'

instruction: How does Blood Moon affect fetchlands and dual lands?
output: Blood Moon makes all nonbasic lands lose their abilities and become Mountains.
