It affects the opponent's graveyard immediately: any card that would go to their graveyard from the first turn onward is exiled instead. Against Oops All Spells, a turn-0 Leyline means their Balustrade Spy mills their library into exile, not the graveyard, and Narcomoeba does not trigger (it triggers from graveyard only). This is why Leyline is the #1 hate card.

instruction: Can my opponent use Karakas on a creature I put into play with Show and Tell?
output: Yes, but not immediately. Show and Tell resolves with all permanents entering simultaneously. After Show and Tell finishes resolving, the active player gets priority. If it is your turn and you cast Show and Tell, YOU get priority first. You could activate your own abilities before passing priority. Then your opponent gets priority and can activate Karakas to bounce your legendary creature. If it is somehow their turn and they get priority first, they can Karakas immediately. The takeaway: on your turn, you get one window of priority before they can Karakas. Use it wisely (attack with Emrakul via Sneak Attack, etc).

instruction: What happens if both players have Orcish Bowmasters in play and one player casts Brainstorm?
output: When a player casts Brainstorm (draw 3 cards), both Bowmasters trigger for each card drawn beyond the first. The Brainstorm caster draws 3 cards: the 2nd and 3rd draws trigger the opposing Bowmasters (2 triggers) AND the caster's own Bowmasters does NOT trigger (it only triggers on opponents' draws). So the opponent gets 2 Bowmasters triggers (2 damage, Army grows by 2). If the Brainstorm caster also had drawn a card earlier that turn (draw step), the first Brainstorm draw is the 2nd draw of the turn, meaning all 3 Brainstorm draws trigger opponent's Bowmasters (3 triggers). This is why Brainstorm against Bowmasters is so painful.

instruction: How does Dark Depths' sacrifice work as a state-based action?
output: Dark Depths has the ability: when Dark Depths has no ice counters on it, sacrifice it. This is NOT a state-based action - it is a triggered ability. When the last counter is removed (by Thespian's Stage copying it with 0 counters, or by Vampire Hexmage removing all counters), the trigger goes on the stack. Players can respond to this trigger. When it resolves, you sacrifice Dark Depths and create Marit Lage, a 20/20 black Avatar creature token with flying and indestructible. You CAN respond: Stifle the trigger (Depths stays with 0 counters but no Marit Lage), Wasteland the Depths before the trigger resolves (no Depths to sacrifice, trigger fizzles... actually, you sacrifice it as the trigger resolves, so Wastelanding in response means it is already gone and the trigger still creates Marit Lage. Wait - the trigger says 'sacrifice it' so if Depths is gone, you cannot sacrifice it, but you still create the token. Actually, re-reading: the ability says sacrifice AND create. If you cannot sacrifice (already gone), you still create the token. So Wasteland does not stop Marit Lage).

instruction: How does Wasteland interact with fetch lands the opponent controls?
output: You can Wasteland an opponent's uncracked fetchland, destroying it before they can search. They lose the land and get nothing. If they crack the fetchland in response to Wasteland, they pay 1 life and search normally while Wasteland's target becomes illegal and the ability fizzles. The correct play is usually to Wasteland during their upkeep or in response to them tapping out, when they might not have priority to crack it first. However, mana abilities (including fetchland activations) can be activated at any time with priority, so they can always respond.

instruction: What is the interaction between Thespian's Stage and Vesuva?
output: Thespian's Stage copies a land by paying 2 and tapping. Vesuva enters as a copy of any land already in play. Both can copy Dark Depths for the Marit Lage combo. The key difference: Stage copies at instant speed (activated ability) while Vesuva copies only on entry. Stage is better because you can hold it and activate at the best moment. With Dark Depths in play, Stage copies it, enters as a Dark Depths with 0 ice counters (Stage had none), triggering the sacrifice and creating Marit Lage.

instruction: Can I use Surgical Extraction on a card that is on the stack?
output: No. Surgical Extraction targets a card in a graveyard, not on the stack. You can only Surgical a card after it has resolved (if it is an instant or sorcery that went to the graveyard) or after a permanent has been destroyed and gone to the graveyard. If your opponent casts Dark Ritual, you cannot Surgical it while it is on the stack. You must wait until it resolves and goes to the graveyard, then Surgical in response to their next spell. Against Doomsday, wait until Doomsday goes to the graveyard after resolving, then Surgical the Oracle from the 5-card pile they just made.

instruction: How does Trinisphere work with alternative costs?
output: Trinisphere makes all spells cost at least 3 mana to cast. This applies AFTER all other cost modifications. Force of Will's alternative cost is exile a blue card + 1 life. With Trinisphere, the total cost must be at least 3, so Force of Will costs exile a blue card + 1 life + 3 mana. Daze's alternative cost is return an Island. With Trinisphere, Daze costs return an Island + 3 mana. Even free spells like Lotus Petal (0 mana) cost 3 mana to cast. Trinisphere is devastating against Legacy's efficient spell base and is a key prison piece in Red Stompy.

instruction: What happens if I exile a card with Imprint but the Imprinted card later leaves exile?
output: Once a card is imprinted (e.g., Chrome Mox), removing it from exile does not change Chrome Mox's function. Imprint creates a link between the artifact and the exiled card. If the exiled card somehow leaves exile (very rare), Chrome Mox no longer has an imprinted card and cannot produce mana. In practice, this almost never happens because cards in exile are extremely difficult to move. The relevant Legacy interaction: if you imprint a card on Chrome Mox and your opponent casts Pull from Eternity to return the imprinted card to the graveyard, Chrome Mox stops producing mana.

instruction: Can you cast a spell during the cleanup step?
output: Normally no. The cleanup step is when you discard to hand size and damage wears off. Players do not normally receive priority during cleanup. However, if a triggered ability triggers during cleanup (e.g., Madness triggers from discarding), the triggered ability goes on the stack, and players DO receive priority. After resolving everything, there is another cleanup step. This means if you have Madness cards and discard to hand size, you can cast them. In Legacy, this is rare but relevant with Faithless Looting discarding Madness cards.

instruction: What is the difference between a spell and a permanent?
output: A spell exists only on the stack while being cast. Once it resolves, if it is a permanent type (creature, artifact, enchantment, planeswalker, land), it becomes a permanent on the battlefield. If it is an instant or sorcery, it goes to the graveyard after resolving. A card is a 'spell' only while on the stack. Force of Will counters SPELLS, not permanents. Abrupt Decay destroys PERMANENTS, not spells. This is why you cannot Force of Will a creature that is already in play - it is no longer a spell. Conversely, you cannot Abrupt Decay a spell on the stack - it is not yet a permanent.

instruction: How does Griselbrand's activated ability work?
output: Griselbrand has: Pay 7 life: Draw 7 cards. This is an activated ability that can be activated at instant speed any number of times as long as you have 7+ life and Griselbrand is in play. After Reanimating Griselbrand (costing 8 life, leaving you at ~12 life), you can activate once to draw 7 (going to 5 life), finding more reanimation spells, countermagic, and threats. If you started at 20 and Reanimate cost 8 (going to 12), you activate twice to draw 14 cards (going to -2... wait, you would die at 0). At 12 life, you activate once (12-7=5 life, draw 7). The life management is critical.

instruction: Does Pithing Needle stop triggered abilities?
output: No. Pithing Needle prevents activated abilities of the named card from being activated. Triggered abilities still trigger normally. Example: naming Aether Vial with Needle prevents the tap ability (putting creatures in play) but does NOT prevent the upkeep trigger (gaining a charge counter). Naming Orcish Bowmasters with Needle does nothing useful because Bowmasters' ability is triggered (when opponent draws), not activated. Naming Thespian's Stage prevents the copy activation (stops the Dark Depths combo). Always name activated abilities, not triggered ones.

instruction: What is the interaction between Daze and Spell Pierce?
output: Both are conditional counterspells. Daze counters unless opponent pays 1 (your cost: return an Island). Spell Pierce counters noncreature spells unless opponent pays 2 (your cost: U). They can be used together for layered disruption: cast Spell Pierce first, if they pay 2, then Daze, and they need another 1 mana (total 3 extra mana to resolve one spell). Or vice versa. Against a turn-2 Show and Tell with opponent having 3 mana, Daze forces them to use their extra mana, then Spell Pierce catches them with no mana left.

instruction: How does the Monarch mechanic work in Legacy?
output: The Monarch is a game designation: at the beginning of your end step, if you are the Monarch, you draw an extra card. You become the Monarch when a card says so (Palace Jailer, Court of Grace) or when a creature deals combat damage to the current Monarch. In Legacy, Monarch is most relevant in Death and Taxes (Palace Jailer exiles a creature and makes you Monarch). The extra card per turn is powerful card advantage. Opponents must attack you with a creature to steal Monarch, which conflicts with blocking strategies. If no creature deals damage to you, you draw an extra card every turn indefinitely.

instruction: Can I Stifle a planeswalker's loyalty ability?
output: No. Planeswalker loyalty abilities are activated abilities, not triggered abilities. Stifle counters triggered abilities and activated abilities. Wait - Stifle says 'counter target activated or triggered ability.' So yes, you CAN Stifle a planeswalker loyalty ability! The loyalty cost (adding or removing counters) is paid as a cost and is not refunded. So if your opponent activates Jace the Mind Sculptor's +2 (gaining 2 loyalty counters), you can Stifle the ability. Jace already gained the counters (cost paid) but the effect (look at top card, put on top or bottom) does not happen. This is a powerful but uncommon play.

instruction: What happens when a player controls no lands and has cards in hand that cost mana?
output: They are effectively locked out until they draw a land or have an alternative way to generate mana. Cards like Lotus Petal, Chrome Mox, Elvish Spirit Guide, and Simian Spirit Guide produce mana without lands. Daze and Force of Will can be cast without mana using alternative costs. In Legacy, the Wasteland + Daze combination can create this scenario: destroy their only land with Wasteland, then Daze any spell they try to cast. If they cannot find another land or free mana, they are soft-locked. This is why running 2-4 basic lands is essential - basics cannot be Wastelanded.

instruction: Does Emrakul's protection from colored spells prevent Jace from bouncing it?
output: Jace the Mind Sculptor's -1 ability says 'return target creature to its owner's hand.' This is an ability of a permanent (Jace), not a spell. Protection from colored SPELLS does not protect against abilities of colored permanents. However, Jace IS a blue permanent. Protection prevents damage, enchanting, blocking, and TARGETING from sources with the protected quality. Since Jace's ability targets Emrakul and Jace is a colored permanent... wait. Protection from colored SPELLS specifically means spells, not permanents or abilities. Jace's ability is not a spell. So Jace CAN bounce Emrakul with the -1. This is why Show and Tell decks fear resolved Jace.

instruction: How does cascade work step by step?
output: When you cast a spell with cascade: 1) The cascade trigger goes on the stack above the spell. 2) Exile cards from the top of your library one at a time until you exile a nonland card with mana value less than the cascading spell. 3) You may cast that card without paying its mana cost. 4) Put all other exiled cards on the bottom of your library in a random order. Important: the cascaded spell resolves BEFORE the original spell because it goes on top of the stack. Cascade cannot find cards with equal or higher mana value. Suspend cards with no mana cost (like Ancestral Vision, MV 0) can be cascaded into because 0 is less than any positive number.

instruction: What is the cascade suspend trick in Legacy?
output: Suspend cards like Ancestral Vision and Crashing Footfalls have no mana cost (mana value 0) and can only be cast by suspend or other effects that let you cast them without paying. Cascade spells exile until hitting a card with lower MV, and MV 0 is lower than anything. So Shardless Agent (MV 3) cascading into Ancestral Vision casts it for free (draw 3 cards) without waiting for suspend. This is the basis of Cascade Crash (Crashing Footfalls = 2 free 4/4 Rhinos) and older Shardless BUG (Agent into Ancestral Vision).

instruction: Can I cast an instant during the resolution of another spell?
output: No. While a spell is resolving, no player has priority and no spells or abilities can be cast or activated (except mana abilities). You must wait until the spell finishes resolving completely, then the active player gets priority. For example, during Show and Tell resolution, you cannot cast an instant between permanents entering play. Everything enters simultaneously, then players get priority. Similarly, while Brainstorm is resolving (drawing 3, putting 2 back), you cannot crack a fetchland in the middle.

instruction: How does Emrakul's extra turn trigger work with Sneak Attack?
output: Emrakul, the Aeons Torn says 'when you CAST Emrakul, take an extra turn after this one.' This is a cast trigger, meaning it triggers only when Emrakul is cast from hand by paying mana. Sneak Attack puts Emrakul onto the battlefield without casting it. Therefore, Sneak Attack does NOT give you an extra turn. Show and Tell also puts Emrakul into play without casting, so no extra turn. The extra turn only happens when you actually spend 15 mana to cast Emrakul from hand (extremely rare in Legacy).

instruction: What happens if I control two Orcish Bowmasters and my opponent draws a card?
output: Each Bowmasters has its own triggered ability that triggers independently. When your opponent draws a card beyond their first of the turn, BOTH Bowmasters trigger. Each trigger amasses Orcs 1 (creating a 1/1 or adding a counter to an existing Army) and deals 1 damage to any target. So with two Bowmasters, each extra draw produces: 2 damage, Army grows by 2. Against a Brainstorm (3 draws), if it is the first draw action: draws 2 and 3 trigger both Bowmasters = 4 triggers = 4 damage, Army grows by 4. Doubled Bowmasters is devastating against cantrip-heavy decks.

instruction: How does Lurrus of the Dream-Den's companion restriction work?
output: Lurrus was a companion that required your starting deck to contain no permanent cards with mana value 3 or more. The companion mechanic was errata'd in 2020: you must pay 3 generic mana to move your companion from outside the game to your hand before casting it normally. Lurrus was banned in Legacy because the restriction was too easy to meet (most Legacy permanents cost 0-2 mana) and the guaranteed card advantage (recast a permanent from graveyard each turn) was too powerful. Yorion (80-card restriction) survives because the deckbuilding cost is more significant.

instruction: What is the difference between hexproof, shroud, and protection?
output: Hexproof: cannot be targeted by opponents' spells and abilities. You CAN target your own hexproof permanent. Shroud: cannot be targeted by ANY spells or abilities, including your own. You cannot equip, enchant, or buff a creature with shroud. Protection from [quality]: cannot be Damaged, Enchanted/Equipped, Blocked, or Targeted by sources of the named quality (DEBT). Protection is the strongest because it prevents damage too, not just targeting. Example: True-Name Nemesis (protection from a player) > hexproof because it also cannot be damaged or blocked by that player. All three can be bypassed by non-targeting effects (Toxic Deluge, Terminus, Liliana sacrifice).

instruction: What happens when you copy a spell with storm?
output: Storm creates copies of the spell for each other spell cast before it that turn. Copies are put directly on the stack - they are NOT cast. This means: copies do not increase the storm count (they are not cast), copies cannot be cascaded (cascade triggers on cast), and copies cannot be countered by Chalice of the Void (Chalice counters cast spells with the matching MV). However, copies CAN be countered by regular counterspells (Force of Will, Flusterstorm). Flusterstorm is particularly good because its own storm creates copies that can counter each storm copy individually.

instruction: What is the legend rule and how does it apply to both players?
output: The legend rule says: if a player controls two or more legendary permanents with the same name, that player chooses one to keep and puts the rest in the graveyard. Crucially, this only applies to permanents YOU control. If you and your opponent both control a Karakas, both stay in play because each player controls only one. If Show and Tell puts your Emrakul and their Emrakul into play, both stay (different controllers). The legend rule only forces a choice when one player controls multiple of the same legend.

instruction: Can abilities be activated while a player is searching their library?
output: No. While a player is searching their library (from a fetchland, tutor, etc.), no player has priority. The searching player finds a card (or fails to find) and the effect finishes resolving. Only then do players get priority again. You cannot activate abilities or cast spells while someone is searching. However, note that the sacrifice of a fetchland happens as a COST before the search begins. So the fetchland is already in the graveyard during the search. If you need to respond to a fetchland activation, you must do so before the ability resolves (before the search starts), not during.

instruction: What is a replacement effect vs a triggered ability?
output: Replacement effects change HOW an event happens. They use 'instead' or 'as' and do not use the stack. Example: Leyline of the Void - cards go to exile INSTEAD of the graveyard. You cannot respond because the event is simply changed as it happens. Triggered abilities respond to events AFTER they happen. They use 'when', 'whenever', or 'at' and go on the stack (can be responded to). Example: Orcish Bowmasters - WHENEVER an opponent draws, trigger. The draw happens, then the trigger goes on the stack. Key difference: you can respond to triggers (Stifle them, kill the creature) but you cannot respond to replacement effects.

instruction: How does priority work in a multiplayer Legacy game?
output: In multiplayer Legacy (rare but exists in some formats), priority passes in turn order starting with the active player. After the active player passes, the next player in turn order gets priority, and so on. All players must pass in succession with an empty stack for the phase/step to end. A spell resolves only after all players pass priority in succession with it on top of the stack. This means the player immediately after the caster gets to respond first, but all players eventually get the chance. In 2-player Legacy (the norm), it is simply active player then non-active player.

instruction: What is a replacement effect for drawing cards?
output: Dredge is the most common draw replacement in Legacy. When you would draw a card and a card with dredge is in your graveyard, you may choose to mill N cards and return the dredge card to hand instead of drawing. Other draw replacements: Notion Thief replaces opponent's extra draws with your draws. Chains of Mephistopheles (Reserved List, very rare) replaces draws beyond the first with discard-then-draw. Spirit of the Labyrinth prevents extra draws entirely (replacement with nothing). These all apply before the draw happens and cannot be responded to since they are replacement effects.

instruction: How does the London Mulligan interact with Leyline of the Void?
output: With the London Mulligan, you draw 7 cards, decide whether to keep, and if you mulligan, you draw 7 again then put cards on the bottom. Leyline of the Void can start in play if it is in your opening hand. The process: 1) Draw 7. 2) If you have Leyline, you may reveal it. 3) Decide keep or mulligan. 4) If mulligan, shuffle all 7 back, draw 7 again. Leyline goes back too and might not be in the new 7. 5) After all mulligans, reveal any Leylines in your final hand. They start in play. The risk: mulliganing aggressively for Leyline means you might end up with a 5-card hand that has Leyline but no functional game plan.

instruction: What is the difference between loses the game and damage?
output: Losing the game is a game action that ends the game immediately for that player. Damage reduces life totals and can be prevented, redirected, or modified. Key distinction: effects that say 'loses the game' cannot be prevented by damage prevention (Solitary Confinement prevents damage but not losing). Poison counters (10 = lose), decking (drawing from empty library = lose), and Pact upkeep costs (cannot pay = lose) all cause game loss without dealing damage. Tendrils of Agony causes loss of life, which is different from damage - loss of life cannot be prevented by damage prevention effects. This is why Tendrils kills through Solitary Confinement.

instruction: Can I sacrifice a permanent I do not own but control?
output: Yes. You can sacrifice any permanent you control, regardless of who owns it. If you Reanimate an opponent's creature from their graveyard, you control it. You can sacrifice it to Goblin Bombardment, Cabal Therapy, or any sacrifice effect. When a permanent you control but do not own would go to the graveyard (from sacrifice, destruction, etc.), it goes to its OWNER's graveyard, not yours. So sacrificing your opponent's Reanimated creature puts it in THEIR graveyard, where they might Reanimate it again.

instruction: How do MDFC (modal double-faced cards) work in Legacy?
output: Modal double-faced cards have two faces and you choose which face to play. Unlike transform cards (Delver of Secrets), MDFCs are not cast and then flipped - you choose which face on casting. Agadeem's Awakening is a sorcery on the front and a land on the back. You can play it as a tapped land when you need mana or cast it as a spell when you need the effect. In Legacy, MDFCs see limited play because both sides are usually weaker than dedicated cards. The flexibility rarely compensates for the reduced power. The most played MDFC in Legacy is Agadeem's Awakening in some black combo lists as a spell that doubles as a land.

instruction: What is the interaction between Stoneforge Mystic and Batterskull?
output: Stoneforge Mystic has two abilities: 1) ETB: search library for an equipment card and put it in hand. 2) 1W, tap: put an equipment card from hand onto the battlefield. The key is the second ability: you search for Batterskull (a 4WW living weapon equipment) with the ETB, then use the tap ability to put it directly onto the battlefield for just 1W on the next turn. This cheats Batterskull's 5-mana equip cost and 5-mana casting cost. Batterskull enters with a 0/0 Germ token that immediately gets +4/+4 and vigilance/lifelink. A 4/4 vigilance lifelink on turn 3 for effectively 3 total mana across 2 turns is excellent.

instruction: What is convoke and is it relevant in Legacy?
output: Convoke lets you tap creatures to help pay for a spell. Each tapped creature pays for 1 generic mana or 1 mana of that creature's color. In Legacy, convoke is most relevant on Hogaak, Arisen Necropolis, which costs 5BG but has both convoke and delve. Hogaak is cast from the graveyard using a combination of tapping creatures (convoke) and exiling graveyard cards (delve). This means Hogaak costs 0 mana in practice - you just need 2 creatures and 5 graveyard cards. An 8/8 trample for free is extremely powerful in the Hogaak archetype.

instruction: How does the Wish mechanic work in tournament play?
output: Wish cards (Burning Wish, Cunning Wish, Living Wish, etc.) say 'you may choose a card you own from outside the game.' In tournament play, 'outside the game' means your sideboard. So Burning Wish searches your sideboard for a sorcery card. In The EPIC Storm, the sideboard contains sorceries for Burning Wish: Tendrils of Agony (win condition), Empty the Warrens (alternate win), Past in Flames (recursion), Pulverize (artifact destruction), etc. The card you Wish for goes to your hand and must be cast normally (paying mana costs). You cannot Wish for cards in exile, in other games, or from a card binder.

instruction: What happens if a creature has both first strike and deathtouch?
output: First strike means the creature deals damage in the first strike combat damage step, before normal combat damage. Deathtouch means any amount of damage dealt by this creature is lethal. Combined: the creature deals first strike damage with deathtouch, killing any creature it damages before that creature can deal normal combat damage back. In practice, a 1/1 first strike deathtouch creature can block and kill any non-first-strike creature without dying. In Legacy, this is relevant on Baleful Strix (1/1 flying deathtouch, also draws a card on ETB) which blocks Murktide Regent profitably.

instruction: Can I use Force of Will to counter a land being played?
output: No. Playing a land is a special action, not a spell. Force of Will can only counter spells (cards that are cast and go on the stack). Lands are played directly onto the battlefield without going on the stack. There is no opportunity to counter a land. To stop an opponent from benefiting from their lands, you need: Wasteland (destroy it), Blood Moon (change it to Mountain), Rishadan Port (tap it), or Stifle (counter an activated ability of the land, like a fetchland search). Preventing land drops requires proactive strategies, not reactive countermagic.

instruction: What is a 'dies' trigger and how does it interact with exile effects?
output: A 'dies' trigger means 'when this creature is put into the graveyard from the battlefield.' If a creature is exiled instead of going to the graveyard (Swords to Plowshares, Solitude, Path to Exile), it does NOT die and 'dies' triggers do NOT happen. However, with Leyline of the Void, creatures that would go to the graveyard are exiled instead, but the game still considers them to have 'died' because the replacement effect changed the destination but not the event type. This is a subtle but important distinction: Swords exiles directly (no death), while Leyline replaces the graveyard destination (still counts as death for triggers).

instruction: How does the Companion mechanic work after the 2020 errata?
output: After the errata: 1) Before the game, reveal your companion from outside the game (sideboard). 2) Your deck must meet the companion's restriction. 3) Once per game, you may pay 3 generic mana to put the companion from outside the game into your hand. 4) Then cast it normally from hand, paying its mana cost. This means companions cost 3 extra mana on top of their casting cost. In Legacy, only Yorion, Sky Nomad sees significant play because the 80-card restriction is manageable and Yorion's blink effect is powerful enough to justify the total 8 mana investment (3 to hand + 3WU to cast). Lurrus and Zirda are banned.

instruction: Can I activate a planeswalker ability the turn it enters?
output: Yes. Planeswalker loyalty abilities can be activated once per turn at sorcery speed (during your main phase when stack is empty). They can be activated the same turn the planeswalker enters the battlefield. There is no summoning sickness for planeswalkers. Example: play Jace the Mind Sculptor (enters with 3 loyalty), immediately activate +2 to go to 5 loyalty or 0 to Brainstorm.

instruction: What is the maximum hand size in Legacy?
output: Seven cards by default. During the cleanup step of each turn, if you have more than 7 cards in hand, you discard down to 7. Some cards modify this: Reliquary Tower gives unlimited hand size, Ensnaring Bridge cares about your hand size for blocking. In practice, most Legacy games do not involve exceeding 7 cards because spells are cheap and you cast them as quickly as you draw them. The exception is after a big card draw spell like Ad Nauseam (draw 10-15 cards, then go off immediately).

instruction: How does cascade interact with Chalice of the Void?
output: The cascaded spell IS cast (cascade says 'you may cast it'), so Chalice can counter it. If Chalice is on 0 and you cascade into a 0-cost spell (Ancestral Vision, Crashing Footfalls), Chalice counters the cascaded spell. This is a common interaction: Eldrazi plays Chalice on 0 against Cascade Crash decks to stop the free Crashing Footfalls. The cascading spell itself (e.g., Shardless Agent at MV 3) is not affected by Chalice on 0.

instruction: What is the interaction between Wasteland and basic land types?
output: Wasteland says 'destroy target nonbasic land.' Basic lands cannot be targeted by Wasteland. However, a nonbasic land WITH a basic land type (like Underground Sea, which is a 'Land - Island Swamp') IS a nonbasic land and CAN be Wastelanded. The basic land types (Plains, Island, Swamp, Mountain, Forest) are different from the basic supertype. Dual lands have basic land types but are NOT basic lands. Only cards with the 'Basic' supertype (Basic Land - Island) are immune to Wasteland.

instruction: Can I equip a creature at instant speed?
output: Normally no. The equip keyword ability is activated at sorcery speed only. However, some cards allow instant-speed equipping: Stoneforge Mystic's second ability puts equipment from hand to battlefield (not equip, but similar), Leonin Shikari gives all equip abilities flash, and Sigarda's Aid auto-attaches equipment when it enters. In Legacy, instant-speed equipment attachment is most relevant with Stoneforge Mystic putting Batterskull into play and it auto-equipping the Germ token (this is ETB attachment, not equip, so it happens at any speed Show and Tell could).

instruction: What happens if a spell resolves but its controller has already lost?
output: If a player loses the game (goes to 0 life, draws from empty library, etc.), they leave the game immediately. All spells and abilities they control are removed from the stack. Their permanents leave the game. In two-player games, this means the other player wins. Spells or abilities controlled by the losing player that are on the stack never resolve. Example: you cast Tendrils of Agony with lethal storm, but in response opponent kills you with Lightning Bolt. You lose, your Tendrils and copies are removed from the stack, and opponent wins (even though they would have died to Tendrils).

instruction: Can I pay costs with opponent's permanents?
output: Generally no. You can only sacrifice permanents you control, tap permanents you control, and pay mana from sources you control. However, some costs specifically let you interact with opponent's permanents: Thoughtseize makes the opponent discard, sacrifice effects like Liliana force the opponent to sacrifice. Convoke lets you tap creatures YOU control. The exception: if you control an opponent's creature (via Reanimate, Control Magic), you CAN sacrifice it because you control it. Costs always refer to things you control unless specifically stated otherwise.

instruction: What is the difference between a token and a card?
output: Tokens are permanents created by effects that are not cards. They can be creatures, artifacts, enchantments, etc. Key differences from cards: 1) Tokens cease to exist when they leave the battlefield (move to graveyard, hand, exile, or library). They briefly exist in the new zone then vanish as a state-based action. 2) Tokens cannot be 'drawn' or 'searched' from a library. 3) Tokens trigger 'dies' abilities (they do go to the graveyard briefly before ceasing to exist). In Legacy, tokens are created by: Orcish Bowmasters (Orc Army), Urza's Saga (Constructs), Batterskull (Germ), Empty the Warrens (Goblins), Marit Lage (from Dark Depths).

instruction: How does damage assignment work with trample?
output: With trample, the attacking creature must assign lethal damage to each blocker before assigning remaining damage to the defending player. Lethal damage = the blocker's toughness minus damage already marked. Example: 8/8 trample blocked by a 3/3. Assign 3 damage to the 3/3 (lethal), remaining 5 tramples over to the player. With deathtouch + trample: since deathtouch means ANY amount of damage is lethal, you only need to assign 1 damage to each blocker (1 is lethal with deathtouch) and the rest tramples. A 1/1 deathtouch trample blocked by a 10/10 assigns 1 to the 10/10 (lethal with deathtouch) and 0 tramples (1/1 has no remaining power).

instruction: Can I respond to my own spell before it resolves?
output: Yes. After casting a spell, you receive priority before passing to your opponent. You can cast another instant or activate abilities before your opponent even gets a chance to respond. This is called 'retaining priority' or 'holding priority.' In Legacy, this is critical for: 1) Casting Lion's Eye Diamond after Burning Wish (crack LED in response to Wish before it resolves). 2) Casting multiple spells in a row to build storm count before opponent can respond. 3) Stacking multiple abilities. You must explicitly announce you are retaining priority; by default, priority passes to the opponent.

instruction: What is the 'Legend Rule' exception for different controllers?
output: The Legend Rule only applies to legendary permanents controlled by the SAME player. If you control Emrakul and your opponent controls Emrakul, both stay in play because different players control them. The rule only forces a choice when one player controls two or more legends with the same name. This matters with Show and Tell: if both players put Emrakul into play, both stay (different controllers). If you somehow put two copies of your own Emrakul into play (very rare), you choose one and the other goes to the graveyard.

instruction: What happens if a triggered ability triggers during the resolution of a spell?
output: The triggered ability waits. During spell resolution, no player has priority and nothing goes on the stack. Once the spell finishes resolving, state-based actions are checked, then triggered abilities that triggered during resolution are put on the stack. The active player puts their triggers on the stack first (in any order), then the non-active player. Then players get priority normally. Example: Balustrade Spy enters and mills your library. Narcomoeba triggers go on the stack AFTER Spy finishes resolving (after the entire library is milled). All Narcomoeba triggers then resolve, putting them into play.
